title: Background Task reload-durability — durable execution vs durable state (scope & guard)
summary: Prep platform-substrate survey for decision #450 (unblocks the durability:reload tier build #134). The Background Task Surface ships a route-only baseline (navigationGuard arms a beforeunload + Navigation-API confirm); durability:reload is the opt-in tier that promises work surviving a full reload. Two coupled forks gate it: (1) scope beyond fetches, (2) does durability:reload relax navigationGuard:warn. The survey settles fork 1 by reshaping it: there is NO native API that runs arbitrary client-side computation in the background across a reload — Background Fetch continues only predetermined transfers while page+SW are closed (Chromium-only); service workers are event-driven and killed at ~30s idle / 5min per event; Background/Periodic Sync are best-effort network-defer; Web Locks die with their context. The only non-fetch option is checkpoint→IndexedDB→resume-on-return (classic checkpoint-restart), which is a categorically WEAKER, different guarantee: durable EXECUTION (work continues while away, Background Fetch) vs durable STATE (work pauses; only a save-point survives; the user resumes on return). Conflating both under durability:reload would overclaim. Recommendation: ship durability:reload as transfer-backed durable execution now; non-fetch compute becomes a distinct future durability:resumable (checkpointed) value, not a rival definition. Fork 2: relax navigationGuard:warn by default when durability:reload is armed, and re-arm the guard on degradation to route-only (work no longer survives). No invented jargon; durable-execution/durable-state framing + checkpoint/state-rehydration vocabulary.
